text: The surgeon places the first two laparoscopic ports and performs extensive lysis of adhesions. The falciform ligament is visible as the surgeon creates space for the third and fourth robotic trocars in the hypogastrium region.

The surgeon places the first two laparoscopic ports and performs an extensive lysis of adhesions. The falciform ligament is visible as the surgeon creates space to place the third and fourth robotic trocars, working in the hypogastrium region.
The surgeon exposes the midline, revealing previously placed proline sutures. Space is created for the placement of the third robotic arm, and the lysis of adhesions continues in the surgical field.

The surgeon exposes the midline, revealing previously placed proline sutures. Space is created for the third and fourth robotic arms. A 12mm trocar is placed in the left upper quadrant, and robotic lysis of adhesions is performed to fully expose the abdomen. The midline, described as having a 'Swiss cheese' appearance, is avoided for closure, with a right upper quadrant trocar site used instead. The midline is placated with a zero V lock suture to close hernia defects without using mesh.

The surgeon places a twelve millimeter trocar in the left upper quadrant, preparing for robotic lysis of adhesions to fully expose the abdomen. The midline shows multiple hernia defects resembling Swiss cheese, which are addressed by placating with a zero V lock suture during closure, avoiding mesh.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to enter the gastrohepatic ligament, exposing the transverse colon tumor. The procedure continues by dissecting the omentum from the transverse colon to fully reveal the tumor, while some omentum remains adherent in the pelvis.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to enter the gastrohepatic ligament, exposing the transverse colon tumor. The omentum is dissected off the transverse colon to fully reveal the tumor. Some omentum remains adherent in the pelvis, which is amputated. The tumor is visible, and the surgeon performs a mesenteric dissection by closely following the bowel wall, opting for a palliative resection due to the patient's comorbidities and previous surgeries.

The surgeon has exposed the obstructing transverse colon tumor and is performing a mesenteric dissection by closely following the bowel wall. A window is created through the mesentery, and the vessel sealer is used effectively. Indocyanine green (ICG) is applied to confirm the integrity of the colon's blood supply. The surgeon prepares to divide the bowel proximally and distally using the right upper quadrant trocar.

The surgeon creates a window through the mesentery and uses ICG to confirm adequate blood supply to the colon. The right upper quadrant trocar is used to divide the bowel proximally and distally. An isoparastaltic anastomosis is lined up, enterotomies are made with scissors, and a white load stapler is used to form a common channel. The common enterotomy is closed with a second layer of sutures, and the area is covered with omentum.

The surgeon lines up an isoparastaltic anastomosis and creates enterotomies using scissors. A white load stapler is used to form a common channel in the right upper quadrant. The common enterotomy is closed with a second layer of sutures, covered with omentum. The dead omentum is resected, and the midline is placated using a zero V-lock suture.
